concepts.name: slack variables
concepts.description: Variables ξi that allow points to violate the margin constraints in support vector classifiers, measuring the degree of overlap between classes.
concepts.existing_quote: Define the slack variables ξ = (ξ1, ξ2, ..., ξN).

paragraphs.text:
This is page 417
Printer: Opaque this
12
Support Vector Machines and
Flexible Discriminants

In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries
for classiﬁcation. Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chap-
ter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable. Here we cover
extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap. These tech-
niques are then generalized to what is known as the support vector machine,
which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in
a large, transformed version of the feature space. The second set of methods
generalize Fisher’s linear discriminant analysis (LDA). The generalizations
include ﬂexible discriminant analysis which facilitates construction of non-
linear boundaries in a manner very similar to the support vector machines,
penalized discriminant analysis for problems such as signal and image clas-
siﬁcation where the large number of features are highly correlated, and
mixture discriminant analysis for irregularly shaped classes.

In Chapter 4 we discussed a technique for constructing an optimal separat-
ing hyperplane between two perfectly separated classes. We review this and
generalize to the nonseparable case, where the classes may not be separable
by a linear boundary.

concepts.name: convex optimization
concepts.description: An optimization problem with quadratic criterion and linear inequality constraints that has a unique global solution.
concepts.existing_quote: This is a convex optimization problem (quadratic criterion, linear inequality constraints), and the solution is characterized in Section 4.5.2.

This is page 417

concepts.name: nonconvex optimization
concepts.description: An optimization problem that may have multiple local minima, resulting from certain formulations like measuring overlap in actual distance from margin.
concepts.existing_quote: However, the first choice results in a nonconvex optimization problem, while the second is convex.

concepts.name: kernel methods
concepts.description: Techniques that produce nonlinear decision boundaries by constructing linear boundaries in transformed high-dimensional feature spaces.
concepts.existing_quote: These techniques are then generalized to what is known as the support vector machine, which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in a large, transformed version of the feature space.

concepts.name: svm for classification
concepts.description: Support vector machine adapted for classification tasks by finding optimal decision boundaries in feature space.
concepts.existing_quote: Computing the SVM for Classification

concepts.name: nonlinear decision boundary
concepts.description: A decision boundary that is not a hyperplane, achieved by SVMs through kernel transformations of the feature space.
concepts.existing_quote: These techniques are then generalized to what is known as the support vector machine, which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in a large, transformed version of the feature space.

concepts.name: margin constraint
concepts.description: Constraints ensuring that all training points are at least distance M from the decision boundary: yi*f(xi) >= M for all i.
concepts.existing_quote: we can find a function f(x) = xT β + β0 with yif(xi) > 0 ∀i

concepts.name: overlap penalty
concepts.description: A penalty term Σξi that measures the total amount of overlap when classes are not perfectly separable.
concepts.existing_quote: There are two natural ways to modify the constraints: ∀i, ξi ≥0, Σξi ≤constant.

concepts.description: A tuning parameter C that controls the tradeoff between margin width and training error in support vector classifiers.
concepts.existing_quote: The tuning parameter of this procedure is the cost parameter C.

concepts.name: loss plus penalty formulation
concepts.description: The reformulation of SVMs as minimizing a loss function (hinge loss) plus a penalty term (regularization), connecting SVMs to other statistical methods.
concepts.existing_quote: This has the form loss + penalty, which is a familiar paradigm in function estimation.

concepts.description: Parameter λ (equal to 1/C) that controls the tradeoff between model complexity and training error in the loss+penalty formulation.
concepts.existing_quote: It is easy to show (Exercise 12.1) that the solution to (12.25), with λ = 1/C, is the same as that for (12.8).

concepts.name: linearly separable
concepts.description: A property of two classes when there exists a hyperplane that perfectly separates them with no overlap.

concepts.name: nonseparable case
concepts.description: A situation where two classes overlap in feature space and cannot be perfectly separated by a hyperplane.

concepts.name: svm path algorithm
concepts.description: An efficient algorithm for fitting the entire sequence of SVM models obtained by varying the cost parameter C, similar to LARS for lasso.
concepts.existing_quote: Here we describe a path algorithm (in the spirit of Section 3.8) for efficiently fitting the entire sequence of SVM models obtained by varying C.

concepts.name: one-versus-one classification
concepts.description: A multiclass strategy where a binary classifier is built for each pair of classes, with the final prediction based on voting.
concepts.existing_quote: A classifier is built for each pair of classes, and the final classifier is the one that dominates the most

concepts.name: overfit boundary
concepts.description: An excessively complex, wiggly decision boundary that fits training data too closely, resulting from large values of the cost parameter C.
concepts.existing_quote: A large value of C will discourage any positive ξi, and lead to an overfit wiggly boundary in the original feature space

paragraphs.text:
This is page 417
Printer: Opaque this
12

Flexible Discriminants

In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries

concepts.name: smooth boundary
concepts.description: A decision boundary with low complexity achieved by small values of ||β||, controlled by a small cost parameter C.
concepts.existing_quote: a small value of C will encourage a small value of ∥β∥, which in turn causes f(x) and hence the boundary to be smoother.

paragraphs.text:
This is page 417
Printer: Opaque this
12
Support Vector Machines and
Flexible Discriminants

In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries
for classiﬁcation. Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chap-
ter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable. Here we cover
extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap. These tech-
niques are then generalized to what is known as the support vector machine,
which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in

concepts.name: epsilon-insensitive loss
concepts.description: A loss function for SVM regression that ignores errors smaller than ε, analogous to how SVMs ignore well-classified points far from the boundary.
concepts.existing_quote: This is an "ε-insensitive" error measure, ignoring errors of size less than ε. There is a rough analogy with the support vector classification setup, where points on the correct side of the decision boundary and far away from it, are ignored in the optimization.
